food_name: Sambal Judes (Extra Spicy Red Chili Condiment)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Indonesian sambal; closest parent 'chili sauce food product' (FOODON_03311737) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers chilli sauces and similar condiments; used as closest parent for sambal-type chili paste. No specific sambal code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code exists for sambal or Indonesian chili condiment. No defensible code available.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 1 tablespoon (15g) used as a condiment alongside rice, noodles, or grilled dishes; the full jar contains 220g.

nutrient_source.title: Peppers, hot chili, red, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170108)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Sambal Judes is a fresh red chili-based condiment. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for raw hot red chili peppers (FDC ID 170108), which represents the primary ingredient. Minor adjustments for added salt (sodium elevated) and possible small additions of shallots and garlic are noted. No direct sambal judes entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved source available for this ingredient profile.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal judes or fresh red chili condiment. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on analogy with raw chili peppers and non-starchy vegetables as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Non-starchy vegetables with minimal carbohydrate content consistently fall in the GI range of 10–20. Glycemic load per 15g serving = (15 × 8.8 / 100) × 15 / 100 ≈ 0.2, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.guidance: Sambal Judes contains approximately 350mg sodium per 100g due to added salt in preparation; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1 teaspoon (5g) per serving to keep sodium contribution below 20mg per use.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and only 8.8g carbohydrate per 100g, Sambal Judes has negligible glycemic impact when used as a condiment in typical serving sizes of 10–15g, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat and cholesterol; capsaicin may have cardioprotective properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sambal Judes is very low in saturated fat (0.04g/100g) and contains no cholesterol; capsaicin from red chili has been associated with anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating effects in peer-reviewed literature, though sodium content warrants moderation for those with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Judes is an Indonesian extra-spicy fresh red chili condiment sold in a 220g jar. The name 'judes' in Indonesian colloquially means fierce or sharp-tempered, reflecting the intense heat level of this preparation. It is made primarily from fresh red chili peppers (cabai merah), typically blended with shallots, garlic, and salt, and may include a small amount of lime juice or vinegar for brightness. The product is rated at spice level 4, indicating very high heat. Texturally, sambal judes is a coarse to smooth paste with a vibrant red colour, pungent aroma, and fiery flavour profile. It is used as a table condiment alongside rice dishes, grilled meats, fried foods, and noodles. Nutritionally, it is low in calories (approximately 40 kcal per 100g), very low in fat, and provides meaningful amounts of vitamin C (143.7mg/100g) and potassium (322mg/100g) from the chili base. Sodium is elevated due to added salt (approximately 350mg/100g), which is relevant for hypertension management. The glycemic index is very low (estimated 15), making it suitable for diabetic dietary patterns when used in typical condiment portions. Capsaicin, the active compound in red chili, has been studied for anti-inflammatory, thermogenic, and potential cardioprotective effects. This product is sold in Singapore through field survey by Smile Martabak - Funan and represents a homemade-style Indonesian sambal.
common_user_queries: Can I eat sambal with high blood pressure?
What is in Indonesian chili paste?
What is the GI of chili sauce?
How much sodium is in sambal?

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for sambal judes exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw hot red chili pepper data (FDC ID 170108), which represents the dominant ingredient. Sodium has been adjusted upward to reflect added salt typical in sambal preparation. GI is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy vegetable category data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary ingredient (fresh red chili) is well-characterised in USDA SR Legacy and the adaptation is defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170108 (raw hot red chili peppers) as no direct sambal judes entry exists in any primary regional database. Sodium elevated to 350mg/100g to reflect added salt in sambal preparation (raw chili baseline is approximately 9mg/100g). 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; plant-based condiment with no hydrogenated fats. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero; plant-based food with no significant vitamin D content. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero; plant-based condiment. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.02), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.24) reported per USDA SR Legacy for raw red chili. 7. GI set to 15 (low) as analogous estimate for fresh chili-based condiment; glycemic load per 15g serving is approximately 0.2, confirming negligible glycemic impact. 8. The product description states 220g jar; serving size set to 220g (full jar) for GL calculation reference, with typical condiment use at 15g per serving. 9. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 A00LC used as closest parent codes; no exact sambal-specific codes exist in either ontology. SNOMED CT has no suitable code — snomed_ct_match_type set to none. 10. Allergen profile: no common allergens identified in standard sambal judes formulation; individuals with chili sensitivity should exercise caution. 11. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — all sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170108 for raw hot red chili peppers.
